questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{x} {/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{(1 - \cos x)(1 + \cos x)}}{{x(1 + \cos x)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{sinx}}{x}.\frac{{\sin x}}{{(1 + \cos x)}} = 0{/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2\sin x - \sin 2x}}{{{x^3}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2\sin x - \sin 2x}}{{{x^3}}}{/tex}is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2\sin x - \sin 2x}}{{{x^3}}} {/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2\sin x(1 - \cos x)}}{{{x^3}}} = 2\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{\sin x}}{x}.\frac{{1 - \operatorname{cosx} }}{{{x^2}}}= 2\times1\times\frac{1}{2} = 1{/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to \pi /4} \;\;\;\frac{{\cos x - \sin x}}{{x - \frac{\pi }{4}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to \pi /4} \;\;\;\frac{{\cos x - \sin x}}{{x - \frac{\pi }{4}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{\cos x - \sin x}}{{x - \frac{\pi }{4}}}{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{4}} \frac{{ - \sin x - \cos x}}{1}= - \sin \frac{\pi }{4} - \cos \frac{\pi }{4}= - \frac{2}{{\sqrt 2 }} {/tex}

questions.question_text: If [x] stands for the integral part of x, then
questions.question_html: If [x] stands for the integral part of x, then
questions.explanation: The [x] is defined as the value which takes the nearest small integer in every integer interval. For example in the interval {tex}0≤x<1{/tex} the value of the function is 0 since it is the lowest integer in that interval . Hence {tex}\lim_{x\rightarrow1^+}{/tex} [x] [ since {tex}1\leq x<2{/tex} ] which gives the value as 1, which is the lowest integer of that interval.

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} {/tex} [sinx] is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} {/tex}[sinx] is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\lim_{x\rightarrow\frac{\mathrm\pi}2}\left[\sin x\right]=1{/tex} [ since {tex}\frac{\mathrm\pi}2-h\leq x\leq\frac{\mathrm\pi}2+h{/tex} ]

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{{x^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{\sin x}}{{2x}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} (Using L’hospital Rule ).

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to - 2} \frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 5} - 3}}{{x + 2}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to - 2} \frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 5} - 3}}{{x + 2}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 2} \frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 5} - 3}}{{x + 2}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 2} \frac{{{x^2} + 5 - 9}}{{(x + 2)(\sqrt {{x^2} + 5} + 3)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 2} \frac{{x - 2}}{{(\sqrt {{x^2} + 5} + 3)}} = \frac{{ - 4}}{{3 + 3}} = - \frac{2}{3}{/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{h \to 0} \;\;\;\left( {\frac{1}{{h\sqrt[3]{{8 + h}}}} - \frac{1}{{2h}}} \right){/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{h \to 0} \;\;\;\left( {\frac{1}{{h\sqrt[3]{{8 + h}}}} - \frac{1}{{2h}}} \right){/tex}is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left( {\frac{1}{{h\sqrt[3]{{8 + h}}}} - \frac{1}{{2h}}} \right){/tex} {tex}=\lim_{h\rightarrow0}\frac1h\left\{\frac12\left(1+\frac h8\right)^{-\frac13}-\frac12\right\}\\{/tex} {tex}=\lim_{h\rightarrow0}\frac1h.\frac12\left\{1-\frac13.\frac h8+\frac{\left(-{\displaystyle\frac13}\right){\displaystyle\left(-\frac53\right)}}2\frac{h^2}{64}+...-1\right\}\\{/tex} {tex}=\lim_{h\rightarrow0}\frac1{2h}\left\{-\frac h{24}+\frac5{18}.\frac{h^2}{64}+...\right\}\\{/tex} {tex}=\lim_{h\rightarrow0}\frac12\left\{-\frac1{24}+\frac5{18}.\frac h{64}+...\right\}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac1{48}{/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to \pi } \;\;\;\frac{{1 + {{\cos }^3}x}}{{{{(x - \pi )}^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to \pi } \;\;\;\frac{{1 + {{\cos }^3}x}}{{{{(x - \pi )}^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \pi } \frac{{1 + {{\cos }^3}x}}{{{{\left( {x - \pi } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{1 + {{\cos }^3}(\pi + h)}}{{{{\left( h \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{1 - {{\cos }^3}h}}{{{{\left( h \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cosh }}{{{h^2}}}.\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} (1 + \cosh + {\cos ^2}h){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{2}(1 + 1 + 1) = \frac{3}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to - 3} \;\;\;\frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 7} - 4}}{{x + 3}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to - 3} \;\;\;\frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 7} - 4}}{{x + 3}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 3} \frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 7} - 4}}{{x + 3}} \end{gathered} {/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 3} \frac{{{x^2} + 7 - 16}}{{(x + 3)(\sqrt {{x^2} + 7} + 4)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 3} \frac{{{x^2} - 9}}{{(x + 3)(\sqrt {{x^2} + 7} + 4)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 3} \frac{{x - 3}}{{(\sqrt {{x^2} + 7} + 4)}}= \frac{{ - 3 - 3}}{{(\sqrt {9 + 7} + 4)}}= \frac{{ - 6}}{8} = \frac{{ - 3}}{4}{/tex}

questions.question_html: If k be an integer, then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to k} \;\;{/tex} (x –[x])

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{x\cos x - \log (1 + x)}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{x\cos x - \log (1 + x)}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{x\cos x - \log (1 + x)}}{{{x^2}}}\end{gathered} {/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{\cos x - x\sin x - \frac{1}{{1 + x}}}}{{2x}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{ - sinx - (x\cos x + \sin x) + \frac{1}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}}}{2}= \frac{1}{2}{/tex} ( using L’Hospital Rule)

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{1-\cos 4x}{{{x}^{2}}}{/tex} {tex} =\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{4\sin 4x}{2x}{/tex} {tex} =\underset{x\to 0}{\mathop{\lim }}\,\frac{16\cos 4x}{2}=8 {/tex} ( using L’Hospital Rule)

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{\cos ec\;x - \cot x}}{x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{\cos ec\;x - \cot x}}{x}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{\cos ecx - \cot x}}{x}{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{x\sin x}}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{(1 - \cos x)(1 + \cos x)}}{{x\sin x(1 + \cos x)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{\sin x}}{x}.\frac{1}{{1 + \cos x}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{x\sin x}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{x\sin x}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{x\sin x}} {/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - {{\cos }^2}x}}{{x\sin x(1 + \cos x)}}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{\sin x}}{x}.\frac{1}{{1 + \cos x}} = 1.\frac{1}{{1 + 1}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\frac{{\tan x}}{{\log (1 + x)}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\frac{{\tan x}}{{\log (1 + x)}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{tanx}}{{log(1 + x)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{tanx}}{x}\frac{x}{{log(1 + x)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{tanx}}{x}.\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{1}{{\frac{1}{x}log(1 + x)}} = 1.\frac{1}{1} = 1{/tex}

questions.question_text: Let f (x) = [x], then f (x) is
questions.question_html: Let f (x) = [x], then f (x) is
questions.explanation: f(x) = [x] is derivable at all x except at integral points i.e. on R – I .

questions.question_text: The function f (x) = [x] is
questions.question_html: The function f (x) = [x] is
questions.explanation: Case 1 Let c be a real number which is not equal to any integer. for all real numbers close to c the value of the function is equal to [c]; i.e., {tex}\lim_{x\rightarrow c}f(x)=\lim_{x\rightarrow c}\left[x\right]=\left[c\right]{/tex} . Also f(c) = [c] and hence the function is continuous at all real numbers not equal to integers. Case 2 Let c be an integer. Then we can find a sufficiently small real number {tex}r>0{/tex} such that [c - r ]= c - 1 whereas [c + r ] = c This, in terms of limits mean that {tex}\lim_{x\rightarrow c^-}f(x)=c-1,\;\lim_{x\rightarrow c^+}f(x)=c{/tex} Since these limits cannot be equal to each other for any c, the function is discontinuous at every integral point.

questions.explanation: Because a polynomial is everywhere differentiable

questions.explanation: Here, f(x) = x|x|, which is product of two continuous functions. Hence, f is continuous {tex}\forall x \in R{/tex} Also, f ' (x) {tex}=x.\frac x{\left|x\right|}+\left|x\right|.1{/tex} {tex}=\left|x+\right|\left|x\right|=2\left|x\right|{/tex} , Therefore f ' (x) exists at all {tex}x \in {\mathbf{R}}{/tex} Further, f ' (0) = 2. |0| = 0

questions.question_text: Let f(x) {tex} = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 1 + x\;if\;x > 0 \\ \;\;x\;\;\;\;if\;x \leqslant 0 \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} {/tex} f(x) is equal to
questions.question_html: Let f(x) {tex} = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 1 + x\;if\;x > 0 \\ \;\;x\;\;\;\;if\;x \leqslant 0 \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} {/tex}f(x) is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} (1 + x) = 1{/tex} {tex}\ \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} x = 0, \\ {/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f(x) {/tex} does not exist

questions.question_text: If f (x) = (1 – x) tan {tex}\frac{{\pi x}}{2}{/tex} , then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 1} {/tex} f(x) is equal to
questions.question_html: If f (x) = (1 – x) tan {tex}\frac{{\pi x}}{2}{/tex}, then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 1} {/tex} f(x) is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 1} (1 - x)\tan \frac{\pi }{2}x{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} ( - h)\tan \left( {\frac{\pi }{2}(1 + h)} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{h}{{\tan \left( {\frac{\pi }{2}h} \right)}} = \frac{2}{\pi }{/tex}

questions.question_text: If both f and g are defined in a nhd of 0; f(0) = 0 = g(0) and f‘(0) = 8 = g’(0), then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{f(x)}}{{g(x)}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If both f and g are defined in a nhd of 0; f(0) = 0 = g(0) and f‘(0) = 8 = g’(0), then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{f(x)}}{{g(x)}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{f(x)}}{{g(x)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{f'(x)}}{{g'(x)}} = \frac{{f'(0)}}{{g'(0)}} = 1{/tex} ( by using L’Hospital Rule )

questions.question_text: If x + |y| = 2y, then y as a function of x is
questions.question_html: If  x + |y| = 2y, then y as a function of x is

questions.explanation: {tex}x+\vert y\vert=2y\Rightarrow x=2y-\vert y\vert{/tex} when {tex}y\geqslant0\;then\;x=2y-y\Rightarrow x=y\;for\;y \geqslant 0\;i.e. y= x\;for\;x \geqslant 0\; {/tex} and when {tex} \;y < 0, then \;x=2y-(-y)\Rightarrow x=3y\;for\;y < 0\;i.e.\;y= 1/3\;x\;for\;\;x < 0.{/tex} We have , {tex}y = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ {\frac{1}{3}x} \end{array}} \right.\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} , \\ , \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x \\ x \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \geqslant \\ < \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0 \\ 0 \end{array}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If f(x) = {tex}x\left( {\sqrt x - \sqrt {x + 1} } \right),{/tex} then
questions.question_html: If f(x) = {tex}x\left( {\sqrt x - \sqrt {x + 1} } \right),{/tex} then

options.text: f (x) is not continuous but differentiable at x = 0
options.html: f (x) is not continuous but differentiable at x = 0

options.text: f (x) is continuous but not differentiable at x = 0
options.html: f (x) is continuous but not differentiable at x = 0

options.text: f (x) is not differentiable at x = 0
options.html: f (x) is not differentiable at x = 0

questions.correct_option_text: f (x) is not differentiable at x = 0
questions.explanation: If f(x) is differential at a point x = a then f(x) is also continuous at x = and left hand limit at that point is equal to right hand limit at that point. And if left hand limit is not equal to right hand limit then function is discontinuous and hence not differentiable at that point. {tex}f(x)=x(\sqrt{x}-\sqrt{x+1}){/tex} We will find the left and right hand limit at x = 0 to check if function f(x) is continuous at x = 0. (L.H.L at x = 0) {tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(-h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}-h(\sqrt{-h}-\sqrt{-h+1}){/tex} As, we can see that square-root of negative number is not defined. So, limit does not exist. (R.H.L at x = 0) {tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} h(\sqrt{h}-\sqrt{h+1}){/tex} = 0 So. here left hand limit at x = 0 is not equal to right hand limit at x = 0. So, the function is discontinuous and also not differentiable at x = 0 Hence, f(x) is not differential at x = 0.

questions.question_text: In case of strict increasing functions, slope of the tangent and hence derivative is
questions.question_html: In case of strict increasing functions, slope of the tangent and hence derivative is
questions.explanation: If f is strictly increasing function , then f ‘ (x) can be 0 also. For example, f(x) = x 3 is strictly increasing, but its derivative is 0 at x = 0. As another example, take f(x) = x + cosx; here f ‘(x) = 1 – sinx, which is either +ve or 0 and the function x + cos x is strictly increasing.

questions.question_text: Let f (x + y) = f(x) + f(y) {tex}\forall {/tex} x, y {tex} \in {\mathbf{R}}{/tex} . Suppose that f (6) = 5 and f ‘ (0) = 1, then f ‘ (6) is equal to
questions.question_html: Let f (x + y) = f(x) + f(y) {tex}\forall {/tex} x, y {tex} \in {\mathbf{R}}{/tex}. Suppose that f (6) = 5 and f ‘ (0) = 1, then f ‘ (6) is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} f'(6) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f(6 + h) - f(6)}}{h}\end{gathered} {/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f(6 + h) - f(6 + 0)}}{h}{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f(6) + f(h) - \left\{ {f(6) + f(0)} \right\}}}{h}{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f(h) - f(0)}}{h} = f'(0) = 1{/tex}

questions.question_text: If x sin (a + y) = sin y, then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If x sin (a + y) = sin y, then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} x\sin (a + y) = \sin y\end{gathered} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = \frac{{\sin y}}{{\sin (a + y)}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\sin (a + y)\cos y - \sin y\cos (a + y)}}{{{{\sin }^2}(a + y)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\sin (a + y - y)}}{{{{\sin }^2}(a + y)}} = \frac{{\operatorname{sina} }}{{{{\sin }^2}(a + y)}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{{\sin }^2}(a + y)}}{{\sin a}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If f (x) {tex} = \frac{1}{{3x + 1}},{/tex} then f ‘ (0)
questions.question_html: If f (x) {tex} = \frac{1}{{3x + 1}},{/tex} then f ‘ (0)
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} f(x) = \frac{1}{{3x + 1}} \Rightarrow f'(x) = \frac{{ - 1}}{{{{(3x + 1)}^2}}}.3 \end{gathered} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow f'(0) = \frac{{ - 3}}{1} = - 3 {/tex} i.e. negative.

questions.question_text: Let f be a function satisfying f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y) for all x, y {tex} \in {\mathbf{R}},{/tex} then f ‘ (x) =
questions.question_html: Let f be a function satisfying f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y) for all x, y {tex} \in {\mathbf{R}},{/tex}then f ‘ (x) =
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} f'(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f(x + h) - f(x)}}{h} \end{gathered} {/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f(x + h) - f(x + 0)}}{h} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f(x) + f(h) - (f(x) + f(0))}}{h} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{f(h) - f(0)}}{h} = f'(0) {/tex}

questions.question_text: Let f and g be differentiable functions such that fog= I, the identity function. If g’(a) = 2 and g(a)=b, then f ‘(b)=

questions.question_text: If f (x) = x tan -1 x then f ‘ (1 ) is equal to
questions.question_html: If f (x) = x tan-1 x then f ‘ (1 ) is equal to

questions.question_text: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}({\tan ^{ - 1}}(\cot x)){/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}({\tan ^{ - 1}}(\cot x)){/tex}is equal to

questions.question_text: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}({\tan ^{ - 1}}(\sec x + \tan x){/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}({\tan ^{ - 1}}(\sec x + \tan x){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}({\tan ^{ - 1}}(\sec x + \tan x)) {/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\sec x\tan x + {{\sec }^2}x}}{{1 + {{(\sec x + \tan x)}^2}}} = \frac{{\sec x(\sec x + \tan x)}}{{2\sec x(\sec x + \tan x)}} = \frac{1}{2}{/tex} . Which is the required solution.

questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\frac{{x({e^{\sin }}^x - 1)}}{{1 - \cos x}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{x({e^{\sin x}} - 1)}}{{1 - \cos x}}\end{gathered} {/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{\frac{{({e^{\sin x}} - 1)}}{x}}}{{\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{{x^2}}}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{({e^{\sin x}} - 1)}}{{\sin x}}.\frac{{\sin x}}{x}.2 = 2 \left( {\because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{2}} \right) {/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to \infty } \;{\left( {1 + \frac{3}{x}} \right)^x}{/tex} is equal to

questions.explanation: We know that, {tex}{\lim _{x \to \infty }}{(1 + \frac{a}{x})^x} = {e^a}{/tex} ....(1) Now, {tex}{\lim _{x \to \infty }}{\left( {\frac{{2x + 3}}{{2x + 1}}} \right)^x} = {\lim _{x \to \infty }}{\left( {1 + \frac{2}{{2x + 1}}} \right)^x} = {e^2},{/tex} by eq(1)

questions.question_text: If x p y q = (x +y) p+q , then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If  xp yq = (x +y)p+q, then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: Given, x p y q = (x +y) p+q Taking log on both sides we get: plogx + q logy = (p + q)log(x + y). Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x we get , {tex}\begin{gathered} \frac{p}{x} + \frac{q}{y}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\end{gathered} {/tex} = (p + q) {tex}\left( {\frac{1}{{x + y}}} \right)\left( {1 + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\left( {\frac{q}{y} - \frac{{p + q}}{{x + y}}} \right)= \frac{{p + q}}{{x + y}} - \frac{p}{x} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\left[\frac{x\left(p+q\right)-p\left(x+y\right)}{x\left(x+y\right)}\right]\left[\frac{y\left(x+y\right)}{q\left(x+y\right)-y\left(p+q\right)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac yx{/tex}

questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\log \left| {\tan \frac{x}{2}} \right|} \right){/tex}is equal to

questions.question_html: The differential coefficient of log (|log x|) w.r.t. log x is

questions.question_text: If x = acos 3 t, y = asin 3 t, then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If x = acos3t, y = asin3t, then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to

questions.question_text: Derivative of sin 3 x w.r.t cos 3 x is
questions.question_html: Derivative of sin3x w.r.t cos3x is

questions.question_text: If {tex}\sqrt x + \sqrt y = \sqrt {a,} {/tex} then {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = a}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If {tex}\sqrt x + \sqrt y = \sqrt {a,} {/tex} then {tex}{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = a}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\sqrt x + \sqrt y = \sqrt a ........(1){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{2\sqrt x }} + \frac{1}{{2\sqrt y }}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - \frac{{\sqrt y }}{{\sqrt x }}.......(2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = - \frac{{\sqrt x \frac{1}{2}{y^{ - \frac{1}{2}}}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - \sqrt y \frac{1}{2}{x^{ - \frac{1}{2}}}}}{x}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ - \left( {\frac{{\sqrt x }}{{2\sqrt y }}\left( { - \frac{{\sqrt y }}{{\sqrt x }}} \right) - \frac{{\sqrt y }}{{2\sqrt x }}} \right)}}{x}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\sqrt x + \sqrt y }}{{2x\sqrt x }} = \frac{{\sqrt a }}{{2x\sqrt x .}} = \frac{{\sqrt a }}{{2a\sqrt a }} = \frac{1}{{2a}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If y 2 = ax 2 + b, then {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If y2 = ax2+ b, then {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}{y^2} = a{x^2} + b \Rightarrow 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2ax \Rightarrow y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = ax{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ax}}{y} \Rightarrow \frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{ya - ax\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}}}{{{y^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{ya - ax\frac{{ax}}{y}}}{{{y^2}}} = \frac{{a({y^2} - a{x^2})}}{{{y^3}}} = \frac{{ab}}{{{y^3}}}{/tex}

questions.question_html: If y = aemx + be-mx, then y2 is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} y = a{e^{mx}} + b{e^{ - mx}} \Rightarrow {y_1} \end{gathered} {/tex} {tex} = am{e^{mx}} + ( - m)b{e^{ - mx}} \Rightarrow {y_2} = a{m^2}{e^{mx}} + ({m^2})b{e^{ - mx}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {y_2} = {m^2}(a{e^{mx}} + b{e^{ - mx}}) \Rightarrow {y_2} = {m^2}y{/tex}

questions.question_text: If y = a sin mx + b cos m x, then {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If y = a sin mx + b cos m x, then {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} y = a\sin mx + b\cos mx \Rightarrow {y_1} \end{gathered} {/tex} {tex} = am\cos mx - bm\sin mx{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {y_2} = - a{m^2}\sin mx - b{m^2}\cos mx{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {y_2} = - {m^2}(a\sin mx + b\cos mx) = - {m^2}y{/tex}

questions.question_text: If {tex}y = \frac{{\log x}}{x},{/tex} then {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = {/tex}
questions.question_html: If {tex}y = \frac{{\log x}}{x},{/tex} then {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = {/tex}
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\frac{{\log x}}{x}} \right) = \frac{{x.\frac{1}{x} - \log x.1}}{{{x^2}}} = \frac{{1 - \log x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\frac{{1 - \log x}}{{{x^2}}}} \right) {/tex} {tex}= \frac{{{x^2}.( - \frac{1}{x}) - (1 - \log x).2x}}{{{x^4}}} = \frac{{ - x - 2x + 2x\log x}}{{{x^4}}} = \frac{{x(2\log x - 3)}}{{{x^4}}} = \frac{{2\log x - 3}}{{{x^3}}}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If f is derivable at x = a , then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to a} \;\;\frac{{xf(a) - af(x)}}{{x - a}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If f is derivable at x = a , then {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to a} \;\;\frac{{xf(a) - af(x)}}{{x - a}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to a} \frac{{xf(a) - af(x)}}{{x - a}} \end{gathered} {/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{(a + h)f(a) - af(a + h)}}{h} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left\{ {\frac{{hf(a)}}{h} - \frac{{af(a + h) -a f(a)}}{h}} \right\} = f(a) - af'(a) {/tex}

questions.explanation: The derivative of an even function is an odd function.

questions.question_text: If {tex}y = \frac{x}{2}\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} + \frac{1}{2}\log (x + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} ),{/tex} then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If {tex}y = \frac{x}{2}\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} + \frac{1}{2}\log (x + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} ),{/tex} then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}y=\frac x2\sqrt{x^2+1}+\frac12\log\left(x+\sqrt{x^2+1}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac12\left[x\frac1{2\sqrt{x^2+1}}\left(2x\right)+\sqrt{x^2+1}\right]+\frac12\left[\frac1{x+\sqrt{x^2+1}}\left\{1+\frac1{2\sqrt{x^2+1}}\left(2x\right)\right\}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac12\left[\frac{x^2}{\sqrt{x^2+1}}+\sqrt{x^2+1}\right]+\frac12\left[\frac1{x+\sqrt{x^2+1}}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{x^2+1}{\displaystyle+}{\displaystyle x}}{\sqrt{x^2+1}}\right\}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac12\left[\frac{x^2+x^2+1}{\sqrt{x^2+1}}\right]+\frac12\left[\frac1{\sqrt{x^2+1}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac12\left[\frac{2x^2+1}{\sqrt{x^2+1}}+\frac{\displaystyle1}{\displaystyle\sqrt{x^2+1}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac12\left[\frac{2x^2+1+1}{\sqrt{x^2+1}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac12\left[\frac{2x^2+2}{\sqrt{x^2+1}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\left[\frac{x^2+1}{\sqrt{x^2+1}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\sqrt{x^2+1}{/tex} . Which is the required solution.

questions.question_text: If {tex}y = x\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} + {\sin ^{ - 1}}x,{/tex} then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If {tex}y = x\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} + {\sin ^{ - 1}}x,{/tex}then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}y=x\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sin^{-1}\left(x\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=x\left\{\frac1{2\sqrt{1-x^2}}\left(-2x\right)\right\}+\sqrt{1-x^2}+\frac1{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{-x^2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}+\sqrt{1-x^2}+\frac1{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{-x^2+1-x^2+1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{-2x^2+2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{dy}{dx}=2\sqrt{1-x^2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: {tex}\frac{{{d^4}}}{{d{x^4}}}({\sin ^3}x){/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{{{d^4}}}{{d{x^4}}}({\sin ^3}x){/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}({\sin ^3}x) = 3{\sin ^2}x\cos x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}}}{{d{x^2}}}({\sin ^3}x) = \frac{d}{{dx}}(3{\sin ^2}x\cos x) = 6\sin x{\cos ^2}x - 3{\sin ^3}x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^3}}}{{d{x^3}}}({\sin ^3}x) = \frac{d}{{dx}}(6{\sin ^2}x{\cos ^2}x - 3{\sin ^3}x){/tex} {tex} = 6{\cos ^3}x - 12{\sin ^2}x\cos x - 9{\sin ^2}x\cos x = 6{\cos ^3}x - 21{\sin ^2}x\cos x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^4}}}{{d{x^4}}}({\sin ^3}x) = \frac{d}{{dx}}(6{\cos ^3}x - 21{\sin ^2}x\cos x) = - 18{\cos ^2}x\sin x - 42\sin x{\cos ^2}x + 21{\sin ^3}x{/tex} {tex} = 60\sin x{\cos ^2}x + 21{\sin ^3}x = - 60\sin x(1 - {\sin ^2}x) + 21{\sin ^3}x{/tex} {tex} = - 60\sin x + 60{\sin ^3}x + 21{\sin ^3}x = - 60\sin x + 81{\sin ^3}x{/tex} {tex} = - 60\sin x + 81\left[ {\frac{{3\sin x - \sin 3x}}{4}} \right] = \frac{{3\sin z - {3^4}\sin 3z}}{4}{/tex}
